title: Diffusio-osmotic transport in nanochannels
abstract: In this chapter, I will enter into the roots of entropically-driven transport with a focus on diffusio-osmotic transport in nanochannels. Diffusio-osmosis is a subtle surface transport, originating in entropic driving forces occuring within the diffuse layers at solid boundaries. Specifying diffusio-osmosis to nanochannels may first look like a marginal refinement, yet it reveals that osmotic drivings can arise in channels and membranes without the prerequisite of semi-permeability, so that diffusio-osmosis extends the domain of existence of entropically driven transport. Osmosis and diffusio-osmosis are two faces of the same phenomenon, naturally embedded in an Onsager framework and quantified by local and global force balances. This perspective clarifies why nanochannels are privileged arenas where diffusio-osmosis and its consequence do flourish. Throughout the chapter, I discuss a set of conceptually relevant examples to show how diffusio-osmosis "pops up" in various situations: as enhanced diffusion, mechano-sensitivity, rectified osmotic flows and, ultimately, as a lever for osmotic energy conversion from single nanopores to membrane modules approaching industrial reality.
summary: This paper explores how concentration gradients drive fluid flow through nanochannels via diffusio-osmosis, a mechanism driven by entropic forces at the interface. It highlights how this effect allows for osmotic energy conversion even in non-selective pores, potentially overcoming the efficiency bottlenecks of traditional membrane technologies. The work connects fundamental thermodynamics with practical applications like blue energy harvesting and ionic diodes.
main_problem: Understanding the fundamental physical mechanisms of diffusio-osmotic transport in nanochannels and how entropic driving forces can be utilized for energy conversion without the need for semi-permeable membranes.
main_result: The paper establishes that diffusio-osmosis extends the domain of osmotic transport to non-selective pores and demonstrates that asymmetric surface charges can induce flow rectification and high power densities for energy harvesting.
method: The author employs the Onsager transport matrix framework, force balance approaches, and the Poisson-Nernst-Planck (PNP) and Stokes equations to model fluid and solute transport.
model_or_system: The system consists of nanochannels (0D pores, 1D nanotubes, and 2D channels) and membranes where transport is driven by concentration gradients and surface-induced entropic forces within diffuse layers.
key_observables: Solvent flow velocity, solute fluxes, electric currents, osmotic pressure, and rectification ratios.
important_parameters: Debye length, Gouy-Chapman length, Dukhin number, surface charge density, and the Staverman coefficient (rejection/selectivity).
main_assumptions: The analysis often assumes a continuum description, neglects the overlap of Debye layers in certain regimes, and assumes small Péclet numbers (neglecting convection in the Smoluchowski equation).
figures_summary: Figure 7 shows a sketch of composite membranes with asymmetric nanopores and a realization using mesoporous polycarbonate and graphene oxide.
paper_structure: The chapter progresses from the fundamental definition of entropic forces to the mathematical Onsager framework, explores specific transport mechanisms (osmosis vs. diffusio-osmosis), discusses molecular-scale refinements like slippage and ion-specificity, and concludes with applications in energy harvesting and osmotic diodes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. : Transport Matrix. Explicit transport matrix L as introduced in Eq.(1), with explanation for each term and colors highlighting the Onsager symmetry. Diagonal terms are associated with permeability (characterizing solvent flux under a pressure drop), diffusion (characterizing solute flux under an applied solute gradient) and electrical conductance (characterizing ionic current under an applied electric field), respectively. The off-diagonal terms correspond to cross ef- fects, with similar mobilities from symmetric terms: diffusio- osmosis (DO) and excess flux under pressure drop; electro- osmosis and streaming currents; diffusio-osmotic currents and excess flux under voltage drop.

FIG. 2. Force balance on diffusio-osmosis. A gradient of solute concentration along a solid surface induces an osmotic pressure drop within the diffuse layer via the interaction with the solid surface. This induces a diffusio-osmotic water flow along the surface.
